verdicts.reasoning: The SQL operation in elFinderVolumeDropbox.class.php line 597 uses $_SERVER['REQUEST_TIME'] which is a PHP-set integer, not user input. The $cursor variable comes from the Dropbox API response, not from HTTP request data. Both values are wrapped in $this->DB->quote() and serialize(). Furthermore, the entire Dropbox volume driver is only instantiated within the authenticated WordPress AJAX handler (fileorganizer_ajax_handler) which requires: (1) authenticated WordPress user via wp_ajax_ hook, (2) nonce verification via check_admin_referer(), and (3) capability check for 'activate_plugins'. The Dropbox driver is not even configured in the free version. No exploitable path exists.

The preg_replace on line 1136 of elFinder.class.php uses pattern '[^0-9a-fA-F]' to strip all non-hex characters from the reqid. The /e modifier (which would allow code execution) was removed in PHP 7.0 and is not used here anyway. The result is a hex-only string assigned to $args['reqid']. This is purely a sanitization operation, not a sink. The taint scanner incorrectly flagged preg_replace as a command injection sink. Additionally, this code is only reachable through the authenticated AJAX handler with nonce and capability checks.

The flow from $args['targets'][0] to $dstVolume->file($dst) on line 1166 is standard elFinder operation. The $dstVolume->file() method looks up file metadata by hash, not by arbitrary file path. The hash is an encoded identifier that maps to files within the configured volume root only. The volume driver validates hashes and restricts access to the configured root directory. There is no file inclusion - file() returns metadata, not file contents via include/require. All access is gated behind authenticated AJAX with nonce and capability checks (activate_plugins).

The call_user_func_array on line 4000 calls $syncCheckFunc which is obtained from $volume->getOption('syncCheckFunc'). This is a server-side PHP configuration option (default: null, set in elFinderVolumeDriver.class.php line 478). It is NOT user-controllable - it can only be set by the plugin developer in PHP code when configuring the volume driver. The $compare argument passed to it is sanitized via intval(). The function name cannot be injected by an attacker. Additionally, is_callable() check is performed before the call. The entire code path requires admin authentication.

The Content-Length header value on line 245 of elFinderConnector.class.php is derived from intval() of regex-matched digits from $_SERVER['HTTP_RANGE']. The $psize variable is computed as ($end - $start + 1), all integer arithmetic. Integers cannot be used for header injection (no CRLF possible). This is also not an open redirect - it's a Content-Length header for HTTP range requests, standard behavior. The code path requires admin authentication through the WordPress AJAX handler.

Same as fileorganizer-open-001. The Content-Range header on line 246 uses integer values ($start, $end, $size) derived from intval() and file size. No injection vector exists as all values are integers. Not an open redirect - this is standard HTTP range response header generation. Requires admin authentication.

The file_get_contents($_FILES['content']['tmp_name']) on line 203 of ZohoOffice/editor.php reads uploaded file content from a PHP temporary file. This is standard PHP file upload handling - $_FILES['content']['tmp_name'] points to a PHP-managed temp file, not a user-controlled path. The content is then written to a volume-managed file identified by hash. The ZohoOffice editor is part of the elFinder editor subsystem, only reachable through the authenticated AJAX handler. The hash-based file targeting prevents arbitrary file write. Additionally, the upload.presave hook in ajax.php performs file type validation and XSS content checking for non-admin users.

summary: All 7 taint flow findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The FileOrganizer plugin wraps the elFinder file manager library behind a properly-secured WordPress AJAX handler (fileorganizer_ajax_handler) that enforces: (1) authenticated users only (wp_ajax_ hook, no wp_ajax_nopriv_ registration), (2) nonce verification via check_admin_referer(), and (3) capability check for 'activate_plugins' (admin-level). There are no REST API routes, no unauthenticated AJAX handlers, and no direct-access PHP files that expose the elFinder connector. The elFinder connector is instantiated in exactly one place (main/ajax.php line 273), inside the protected handler. The taint scanner's findings relate to internal elFinder library operations (SQL queries in Dropbox driver, preg_replace sanitization, call_user_func_array with server-side config values, standard HTTP range headers, and PHP temp file reads) - none of which are reachable without admin authentication, and none represent actual vulnerabilities even within the authenticated context.
feedback: The automated taint analysis suffered from two main issues: (1) The attack surface scanner reported 0 PHP files / 0 lines, suggesting it failed to parse this plugin, yet the taint scanner found 38 files - this inconsistency should be investigated. (2) The taint scanner flagged internal library code (elFinder) without considering the WordPress authentication wrapper. preg_replace was incorrectly classified as a command injection sink (the /e modifier does not exist in this code and was removed in PHP 7). call_user_func_array was flagged as RCE but the function name comes from server config, not user input. HTTP range header values (integers) were classified as open redirect. The scanner should track authentication context from WordPress hooks (wp_ajax_ vs wp_ajax_nopriv_) to avoid flagging admin-only functionality as unauth-accessible.
